title: How HubSpot Scaled Semantic Search to 20 Billion Vectors
gist: HubSpot scaled an internal Vector-as-a-Service platform on top of Qdrant to over 20 billion vectors serving 38+ teams for agents, RAG, and deduplication, and found that the hard problem was not the vector database itself but building Kubernetes-operator automation around it to keep operations stable as demand rose.

insights.claim: HubSpot's internal semantic search platform grew from a proof of concept into a service managing more than 20 billion vectors across 38-plus internal teams.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: Signals mature, production-scale internal AI infrastructure at a SaaS incumbent, a template competitors and vector-DB vendors will benchmark against.
insights.quote: grew from a proof of concept into an internal service that now manages more than 20 billion vectors across 38-plus teams
insights.locator: SaaS software vendor HubSpot has described

insights.claim: HubSpot's vector platform spans more than 200 indexes, 140-plus clusters, five regions, and two environments, with write traffic peaking at 100,000 requests per second.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: Concrete scale metrics reveal the operational footprint required to run agent/RAG retrieval in production, informing infra cost and capacity models.
insights.quote: more than 200 indexes, 140-plus clusters, five regions, and two environments, with write traffic peaking at 100,000 requests per second
insights.locator: HubSpot also says it runs Qdrant in-house

insights.claim: The platform now supports agents, RAG, and contact deduplication, and rising agent usage has made retrieval quality and latency more important than before.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: Agent workloads are the demand driver raising the bar on retrieval SLAs, a leading indicator for demand in the vector search and inference stack.
insights.quote: the increase in agent usage has made retrieval quality and latency more important than before
insights.locator: The company says the system now supports agents

insights.claim: HubSpot chose Qdrant because it can run on-premises and supports named vectors, hybrid search, multi-stage querying, weighted reranking, and cost controls including quantisation and on-disk storage.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: Feature checklist that won a marquee enterprise deployment is a buying-criteria signal favoring self-hostable vector DBs over managed-only rivals.
insights.quote: supports features such as named vectors, hybrid search, multi-stage querying, and weighted reranking, and offers cost controls including quantisation and on-disk storage
insights.locator: The post on HubSpot's site explains

insights.claim: HubSpot runs Qdrant in-house to integrate with internal tracing, cost tracking, rate limiting, scaling, and security tooling while keeping control over customer data.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: Data-control and tooling-integration motives push large enterprises toward self-hosting, a headwind for pure SaaS vector-DB revenue among the biggest accounts.
insights.locator: HubSpot also says it runs Qdrant in-house because

insights.claim: VaaS (Vector as a Service) sits in front of Qdrant and provides access control, embeddings generation, data versioning, and feedback collection.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: The value-add layer wrapping a raw vector DB is where enterprise platform teams invest, defining what commercial vendors must bundle to stay relevant.
insights.quote: called VaaS or Vector as a Service, sits in front of Qdrant and provides access control, embeddings generation, data versioning, and feedback collection
insights.locator: The post on HubSpot's site explains

insights.claim: HubSpot moved from Helm to an internal Kubernetes Operator framework because Helm could not make API calls, auto-scale from external metrics, or handle complex state-aware lifecycle work.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: Marks the tooling frontier for scaling stateful AI infra; watch operator-framework and platform-engineering vendors as beneficiaries of this shift.
insights.quote: Helm could not make API calls, auto-scale from external metrics, or handle more complex state-aware lifecycle work
insights.locator: In the post, Oleg Tereshin and Xin Liu write

insights.claim: HubSpot's Translators reconcile desired and actual system state every 60 seconds, automating cluster creation and decommissioning, shard movement, and replication recovery.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: A concrete reconciliation-loop pattern for operating vector fleets that others will copy, reducing headcount needs per unit of scale.
insights.quote: reconcile the desired and actual state of the system every 60 seconds

insights.claim: Automation cut cluster spin-up time from hours to minutes and removed the need for standby clusters, while handling horizontal scaling, shard rebalancing, and replication recovery.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: Eliminating standby capacity is a direct infra cost reduction, quantifying the efficiency payoff of operator-based automation.
insights.quote: reduced cluster spin-up time from hours to minutes and removed the need for standby clusters
insights.locator: HubSpot also says the shift reduced

insights.claim: Manual cluster management became untenable as the fleet grew, per HubSpot engineers who state that manual operations do not survive growth.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: Reinforces that operational automation, not the database, is the binding constraint on scaling retrieval infrastructure.

insights.claim: Some HubSpot collections contain billions of points, and even one imbalanced shard can force a cluster to scale before it really needs to.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: Shard skew is a hidden cost driver at scale; balancing and memory-skew avoidance are where efficiency and margin are won.
insights.quote: even one imbalanced shard can force a cluster to scale before it really needs to
insights.locator: The company says some collections contain billions of points

insights.claim: Pinecone practitioners describe scaling from 40 million to 600 million vectors with advice that recall must stay high, storage and compute should be separated, and reliability treated as a feature.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: Separation of storage and compute is the converging architectural bet across vector vendors, a design axis to evaluate competing platforms on.

insights.claim: InfoQ has recently covered vector search additions in OpenSearch, PlanetScale, and AlloyDB, signalling developer demand for semantic retrieval close to their data with control over cost and latency.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: Incumbent databases embedding vector search commoditises the capability, pressuring standalone vector-DB pure-plays on differentiation.
insights.quote: developers want semantic retrieval close to their data, with enough control to tune cost and latency
insights.locator: The broader pattern is visible across the vector search market

insights.claim: The main lesson is that once retrieval became central to multiple AI products, the vector database was only part of the problem and the harder task was building automation around it to stay stable as demand rose.
insights.speaker: Matt Saunders
insights.so_what: Reframes vector-search value from the DB engine to the surrounding platform automation, shifting where durable moats and investment should sit.
insights.quote: the database itself was only part of the problem
insights.locator: For HubSpot, the main point is not that vectors are new
